title: SEGMENT 4 — The unc/lin/mab gene namespace and forward genetic screens

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — The unc/lin/mab gene namespace and forward genetic screens
Here's the basic technique of forward genetics, which is the engine that powered most of what came out of the worm in its first two decades.
The logic goes like this.
You have an animal with normal anatomy and behavior.
You introduce mutations at random — typically by feeding the animals a chemical mutagen like EMS, ethyl methanesulfonate, which causes point mutations roughly uniformly across the genome.
You let the mutagenized animals reproduce.
You screen the resulting offspring for any that look or behave abnormally.
Then, having found an interesting mutant, you ask: what gene was mutated?
You map the mutation, you clone the gene, and now you have identified, by phenotype-first reasoning, a gene that's required for whatever process you were screening for.
This is forward genetics, and the name comes from the direction of the inference: you go from phenotype to gene.
The opposite — pick a gene first, knock it out, and see what happens — is reverse genetics, which we'll get to later.
In worms, forward genetic screens were so productive that within a few years there were dozens, then hundreds, of named mutants.
To keep track of them, the worm community settled on a beautifully systematic naming convention.
Every gene gets a three-letter code that describes the phenotype its mutants display, followed by a hyphen and a number.
The number is just the order of discovery within that phenotype class.
So:
unc genes** — "uncoordinated.
" Mutants in unc genes have abnormal movement.
They twitch, they coil, they paralyze, they back up when they should go forward.
There are hundreds of unc genes: unc-1, unc-2, unc-3, on up.
Each one encodes a protein required for some part of the motor or neural machinery — ion channels, neurotransmitter receptors, structural proteins of muscle, components of the synaptic vesicle cycle.
The unc class is, in effect, a genetic dissection of the worm's nervous system and musculature, named by the visible phenotype the mutants display.
lin genes** — "abnormal cell lineage.
" Mutants in lin genes have defects in the stereotyped cell-lineage program.
Specific cells fail to divide when they should, or divide when they shouldn't, or adopt the wrong fate.

The lin class is the genetic toolkit of developmental cell-fate decisions, and includes some of the most famous worm genes — lin-4 and lin-14, for instance, which were the first microRNA and its target ever discovered, leading to a whole new field of small-RNA biology.
mab genes** — "male abnormal.
" Mutants in mab genes have defects specific to males — abnormal tail anatomy, mating defects.
Because the worm is hermaphroditic but produces occasional males, you can specifically study male-only structures and male-only behaviors.
dpy genes** — "dumpy.
" Mutants are short and fat, instead of long and slender.
Often involved in cuticle structure.
let genes** — "lethal.
" Mutants die.
Often used for essential genes where the only phenotype is death.
clk genes** — "clock.
" Mutants have altered developmental timing or lifespan.
age genes** — affecting lifespan.
We'll come back to these in Segment 6.
daf genes** — "dauer-formation abnormal.
" Worms can enter a developmentally arrested, stress-resistant state called the dauer larva when conditions are bad.
daf mutants either fail to enter dauer or enter dauer when they shouldn't.
Some of the daf genes turned out to control aging, and we'll meet them in Segment 6.
The analogy: the worm gene namespace is a phenotype-first dictionary of an animal's genome.
Every gene name tells you, at a glance, what category of phenotype its mutants display.
You read "unc-13" and you know immediately: this is a gene whose mutants are uncoordinated, and there are at least twelve other unc genes that came before it in the discovery order.
The naming convention is a kind of compressed scientific history.
Every name encodes both a phenotype and a place in the chronology.

Compare this with the human gene naming convention, which is a chaotic mess of abbreviations from different historical periods, different naming committees, different fields claiming the same gene under different names.
The worm community, because it was small and started fresh, was able to enforce a single convention from the beginning.
The result is that a worm geneticist can read a gene list and immediately understand what kind of biology is being studied.
Few other organisms have anything like this clarity.
The mechanics of a forward screen in worms look something like this.
You take a few thousand hermaphrodite worms, you feed them mutagen for a few hours, you wash them off, and you put them back on plates to let them recover.
The mutagen has introduced random point mutations throughout the genome — usually on the order of one mutation per ten million base pairs, so each treated worm carries a handful of new mutations.
The treated worms self-fertilize, and their progeny are the F1 generation — each F1 carrying a unique set of mutations from one mutagenized parent.
The F1 generation looks normal, because most mutations are recessive and the F1 worms are heterozygous for them.
You let the F1 worms self-fertilize.
Their progeny — the F2 generation — segregate the mutations.
About a quarter of the F2 from each F1 will be homozygous for any given new mutation.
So in the F2 generation, recessive phenotypes finally appear.
You scan plates of F2 worms looking for anything weird — anything paralyzed, anything misshapen, anything that doesn't behave right.
Any animal that catches your eye is a candidate mutant.
You pick the candidate, you let it self-fertilize, and you check whether the phenotype breeds true.
If it does, you've isolated a heritable mutation in some gene.
Then comes the hard part: mapping it.
Where in the genome is the mutation?
Which gene is broken?
In the 1970s and 80s, this was done by careful genetic crosses against marker strains, narrowing the mutation to a chromosomal region, then to a small interval.
By the 1990s, with the worm genome sequenced, you could combine genetic mapping with sequencing to pinpoint the mutation at single-base resolution within a few months.
By the 2010s, with high-throughput whole-genome sequencing, you can identify the mutation in a new mutant within a few weeks.
What did all this produce?
A few examples will give you the flavor.
unc-86** — a gene whose mutants have defects in specific neuronal lineages.
It encodes a POU-homeodomain transcription factor that's a master regulator of neuronal identity.
The mammalian homolog, Brn-3, plays the same role in mammalian neuronal specification.
The neural fate-determination logic worked out in worm transfers, with modification, to mouse.
lin-14** — a gene whose mutants get developmental timing wrong.
It encodes a protein regulated by a tiny RNA, lin-4, which doesn't encode a protein at all.
lin-4 binds to the 3' UTR of lin-14 mRNA and represses translation.
This was the first microRNA ever discovered.
Today there are hundreds of microRNAs known in humans, controlling everything from cancer to development.
The whole field started with a forward screen for developmental timing mutants in worms.
unc-13** — mutants are paralyzed because they have defects in neurotransmitter release.
The gene encodes a vesicle-priming protein essential for synaptic transmission.
The mammalian homolog, Munc13, plays the same role in mammalian synapses.
The molecular machinery of synaptic transmission, much of it, was worked out in worm.
The big idea to leave this segment with: the worm provided the first genome-wide phenotype-to-gene dictionary of an animal.

Every category of biology — movement, development, sex determination, aging, sensory perception, learning — has its own collection of unc, lin, mab, daf, clk genes, named for the phenotype they break.
That collection has been the substrate for forty years of mechanistic biology, much of which has translated, gene by gene, to vertebrates and humans.
Now let's go to the discovery that probably did more for the field than any other in the last twenty-five years — the discovery of RNA interference, which happened in worm and changed everything.
